locations.preview: But, tasks that are difficult for humans are not necessarily difficult for recent systems: the challenging MMLU or GSM8k benchmarks for example (Hendrycks et al., 2021; Cobbe et al., 2021) are already close to be solved,1 due to rapid LLM improvement possibly combined with data contamination.2 Furthermore, open-ended generation generally requires human or model-based evaluation (Zheng et al., 2023).
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: The results demonstrate that solving complex tasks is challenging: our best GPT-4-based agent only achieves an end-to-end task success rate of 14.41%, significantly lower than the human performance of 78.24%.
locations.title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents

locations.preview: For the dataset release, we plan to open source the SWE-bench task instances, the collection and evaluation infrastructure, the experimental results, the training data used for fine-tuning SWE-Llama models, and the SWE-Llama model weights.
locations.title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?

locations.preview: Directions to evaluate LLMs more holistically, on their broader conversational aspects, have included (i) compilations of evaluations (Gao et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2022a; Srivastava et al., 2023), which are often difficult to aggregate meaningfully and are prone to contamination through data leakage, (ii) human evaluation, which is time-consuming and difficult to scale, or (iii) model based evaluation to overcome this limitation (Zheng et al., 2023).
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: Experiment results show that the best GPT-4 agent performance is somewhat limited, with an end-to-end task success rate of only 14.41%, while the human performance is 78.24%.
locations.title: WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents

locations.preview: We report results for models using different retrieval mechanisms and prompting styles, then provide some analysis and insight into model performance and difficulty.
locations.title: SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?

locations.preview: However, this latter solution relies on using a more capable LLM (often GPT4) than the one currently evaluated, and the quality of the evaluation is affected by the shortcomings of the evaluator LLM, which are not always obvious and can lead to subtly incorrect results.

locations.preview: While the questions are conceptually simple, annotators might do inadvertent mistakes: we estimate the annotator’s success rate to be 92% when aggregated on all levels of difficulty, and report this as the human score for GAIA.

locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

locations.preview: We loosely use the following definitions to attribute a level to a question: - Level 1 questions generally require no tools, or at most one tool but no more than 5 steps. - Level 2 question generally involve more steps, roughly between 5 and 10 and combining different tools is needed. - Level 3 are questions for a near perfect general assistant, requiring to take arbitrarily long sequences of actions, use any number of tools, and access to the world in general.
locations.title: GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants

arguments.markdown:
Based on the headline model success percentages reported in each paper's main results:

**Easiest → Hardest:**

1. **GAIA (easiest)** — GPT-4 equipped with plugins: **15%** overall success rate. On the easiest Level 1 subset, the best model reaches ~30%. [[evidence_id]]

2. **WebArena (middle)** — Best GPT-4-based agent: **14.41%** end-to-end task success rate. [[evidence_id]]

3. **SWE-bench (hardest)** — Best performing model (Claude 2) with BM25 retrieval: **1.96%** resolve rate. [[evidence_id]] With the "oracle" retrieval setting, Claude 2 only climbs to 5.9% and GPT-4 to 3.4%. [[evidence_id]]

So the gap between GAIA/WebArena and SWE-bench is roughly an order of magnitude: GAIA and WebArena are comparably difficult for frontier models (≈14–15%), while SWE-bench is dramatically harder (under 2% for the best model in the standard retrieval setting).
